text:
1. Leviathans. Sons of the Thousand-Mouthed Levianoch, the sea’s ever-hungry beasts... Caleb turned the page. Mythology did not concern him just now. The existence of leviathans was fact, shored up by countless sailors’ accounts, and fifty years ago a captain from Einfjalla had even brought one down in the North. Its skull still sat in the local Museum of Natural History. Caleb had seen it once in person. Magnificent, and easy to imagine a single bite staving in a hull.

What he needed were notes on their habits. They were rare. Predators, without question, and by the reports, territorial. He sighed, glanced at his list, and drew a question mark beside leviathan. Possible, yes, yet it did not feel right, unless many leviathans had agreed to hunt shipping in cycles across every reach of the known world. Besides, every book so far insisted that for all their size and danger they were, in the end, still animals.

He stood and studied the huge calendar spread across the floor one last time, the last five years of disappearances marked to the day. The where did not matter; their correspondents all reported the same situation. Losses spiked in the weeks before every third new moon. In the one or two weeks after new moon, only an occasional ship went missing. Until the past year, when the pattern broke and the clusters of dots evened out.

Two years ago Lord Ebbing, head of the Oceanographic Institute, had convinced the shipping companies and even Parliament to send out fleets only in the days just after every third new moon. Those who took his advice sailed safely from the Colonies to Rivenstorm. Whatever caused the cycle adapted to the new order. That frightened Lord Ebbing, and it frightened the companies as well.

He should have been working on the speech. Caleb was Lord Ebbing’s assistant, more and more his right hand as the old scholar aged. The rest of the Institute mostly pursued their own lines of research and traveled constantly, logging weather, life in the sea, and now, with the right instruments, chemical compositions. A few braver scholars even tried to chart the oceans’ geography, the undersea ridges and trenches, though it was dangerous work.

Caleb had stayed at the Academy with Lord Ebbing. Everything about the sea drew him, which was part of the problem. He could never choose a single narrow field, and he knew that any choice would mean shipping out. Not that he did not want to. In truth he wanted nothing more than to cut the waves on a fast ship. His parents had always been afraid of the water and had made him promise he would never go to sea. He suspected it was because his father had nearly drowned once in a wreck. His heart still ached for it, and somewhere inside he knew he would break that promise one day. Just not today.

It helped that the work here mattered. Perhaps he and Lord Ebbing could save lives. Perhaps his life’s work would be more than another stack of dusty volumes in the Academy’s great library.

He folded the great calendar with care and slid it into an oversized briefcase. Lord Ebbing’s hearing was set for the next day, and Caleb had to attend. He would have to lay out the situation and explain why they could not yet stop it. They had gone over it all dozens of times, yet he always felt it was not enough. If he were on the other side, if it were his ships vanishing and his people lost to the sea, he would want to know why.

It was late, and he should have gone home hours ago. Instead he unlatched the window for a breath of air. The autumn chill bit. Mist sifted down, and through it the city’s lamps burned like will-o’-the-wisps. Music drifted from a nearby dance hall in torn scraps of melody that bled into the halls of the Rivenstorm Scientific Academy. He had no idea whether anyone besides the night cleaner and the porter was as foolish as he was, working at this hour.

He pulled the Encyclopedia of Marine Megafauna back toward him and, after the leviathans, turned to the krakens. Everyone knew octopods were surprisingly clever animals. He rubbed his face. His eyes burned from the print, but he could not stop yet. There was too much to do. What harm to rest his forehead on the desk for a moment?

When the cuckoo clock sounded, Caleb jerked awake and almost swept his papers to the floor. He grabbed the stack on instinct, trying to preserve the fragile order he had managed to impose. He glanced at the time, then scooped up the pages and his little leather notebook and shoved them into the briefcase.

Eight o’clock already. The Western Shipping Company had set Lord Ebbing’s hearing for eight-thirty.

He had planned to go home early, sleep, rise with the sun, bathe, and shave. The research had stretched. Seven became ten, ten became midnight, then four in the morning, when sleep took him at his desk. The committee would get, instead of a neat, well-rested oceanographer, an ink-stained, bleary-eyed, stubbled man who looked more like a feckless student than a serious scholar under Lord Ebbing’s wing. He should at least have changed his shirt. He did not even know why he never kept a spare at the office.

He pelted down the white stairs, worn mirror-smooth, nearly bowling over two quarreling scientists, then burst into the fog with hurried apologies.

October mornings in Rivenstorm were always fogbound, casting a ghostly pall even at noon over the West Coast’s largest port city. Caleb hunched into his brown leather coat and realized he had left his hat upstairs. He hated hats anyway, and it would not have improved matters. Better to focus on not being late. Or not very.

He left the domed building with its now-green copper and its procession of white columns. It had stood for two centuries without flinching, a monument to knowledge and research, the foundation of progress. More than a hundred scholars called it a second home. Its lecture hall and library rivaled the university’s, and each floor housed a different branch of the natural sciences. In time a little quarter had grown up around it to serve the staff. Restaurants, cafés, shops. Owners and Academy people often knew one another by name, not just by face.

He did not dare stop at the bakery that lured with the smell of fresh bread, coffee, and sweets, though he was hungry. He strode past a café where chairs and tables were being set out, then past the little bookshop.

“Running late again, Mr. André?” called the old woman by the greengrocer’s.

“I only ever need to be somewhere I’m not, Mrs. Mellow,” Caleb said, panting, a tired smile on his face.

Mrs. Mellow snagged a shiny red apple from the counter and tossed it to him.
